description: Reference calibration points for the LLM taste scorer. User's stated taste: Martian/LOTR/Heat = high (8-10); Fast 5/Grey Man = low (1-3). Expand via the Upgrades UI — version bump triggers rescore of the library.

high.title: The Martian
high.rationale: Ridley Scott directorial maturity + Dariusz Wolski's meticulous Mars cinematography. The HAB interiors, dust storms, and launch sequences were designed for scale; streaming compression robs them of practical-set detail. Strong Atmos remix with discrete object placement for wind, debris, and radio comms.

high.title: The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring
high.rationale: User-flagged canonical reference. Howard Shore score, Andrew Lesnie photography, 80% practical-effects production. The Balrog, Moria, and Amon Hen sequences are landmark sound design. Atmos remix is a genuine fidelity upgrade over the original 5.1, not a marketing gimmick.

high.title: The Lord of the Rings: The Two Towers
high.rationale: Helm's Deep battle is a demo-disc benchmark — directional rain, layered ambient war sound, discrete object-based thunder. Middle entry of a canonical trilogy.

high.title: The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King
high.rationale: Pelennor Fields charge, lighting of the beacons, and Mount Doom finale are reference-grade cinematography + sound. The Extended Edition UHD remaster genuinely reveals previously-buried grain structure and colour intent.

high.title: Heat
high.rationale: Mann's obsession with urban geometry, Dante Spinotti's LA night photography, Elliot Goldenthal score. The Downtown bank-heist shootout is landmark sound design — discrete gunshot positioning with room-scale reverb. Every subsequent transfer has revealed more; there's craft underneath to unearth.

high.title: 2001: A Space Odyssey
high.rationale: Possibly the purest case for fidelity investment ever committed to film. 70mm source, slit-scan Stargate, Kubrick's obsessive composition. Absence of dialogue means sound design and score (Ligeti, Strauss) carry structural weight. Recent restorations reveal original colour timing and grain.

high.title: Blade Runner 2049
high.rationale: Deakins cinematography (Oscar-winning), Hans Zimmer + Benjamin Wallfisch score, Villeneuve's sustained long takes. Colour-graded for HDR from day one — SDR presentations literally discard tonal information. Atmos mix uses silence and bass extension deliberately.

high.title: Apocalypse Now
high.rationale: Storaro's photography, Walter Murch's sound design (invented surround mixing conventions still used today). The Ride of the Valkyries helicopter assault is a foundational sound-design text. Final Cut restoration preserves original 70mm intent.

high.title: Mad Max: Fury Road
high.rationale: 90% practical stunts, Junkie XL score, John Seale photography. Colour-graded extremely aggressively for specific intent — accurate reproduction matters. Sound mix is dense, layered, and directional throughout. Few recent action films reward audio investment as directly.

high.title: Dune: Part Two
high.rationale: Greig Fraser cinematography, Zimmer score with sub-sonic depth, Atmos mix with extensive vertical use. IMAX-originated sequences carry 1.43:1 framing preserved on UHD. Sandworm sequences are built for subwoofer calibration.

high.title: Interstellar
high.rationale: Hoyte van Hoytema IMAX cinematography, Zimmer pipe-organ score with aggressive dynamic range (controversial but intentional). Docking sequence sound design. Nolan's insistence on 35mm/IMAX source pays off in home presentation when delivered on UHD.

high.title: Children of Men
high.rationale: Lubezki's signature long takes (the car-ambush and Bexhill one-shots). Intimate directional sound design. Modest budget, zero CGI vanity — craft over spectacle. A masterclass in showing that big budgets aren't what makes a film fidelity-worthy.

high.title: Lawrence of Arabia
high.rationale: Shot in 65mm by Freddie Young. The 4K restoration is a revelation — micro-detail in fabric, grain, desert horizons. Maurice Jarre score. One of the handful of films where the difference between 1080p and 4K is genuinely structural, not cosmetic.

high.title: The Revenant
high.rationale: Lubezki natural-light photography (Oscar-winning), long-take structure, Ryuichi Sakamoto score. Bear attack and river sequences use directional sound extensively. The image was built for big screens and large-dynamic-range displays.

high.title: Inception
high.rationale: Wally Pfister cinematography, Zimmer score (the BRAAM was invented here), Paris-folding and hotel-corridor sequences are landmark VFX/sound combinations. Atmos remix was a meaningful upgrade.

high.title: Gravity
high.rationale: Lubezki zero-gravity long takes, landmark use of silence in space (the sound mix pointedly honours vacuum where lesser films cheat). The opening 17-minute continuous shot demands a pristine source to read correctly.

high.title: The Tree of Life
high.rationale: Lubezki natural-light photography, Alexandre Desplat score, formalist Malick direction. The cosmology sequence combines IMAX-scale imagery with orchestral layering — a film built for the biggest presentation you can offer it.

Roger Deakins photography constructed as apparent single take. Sound mix uses directional cues for threats off-screen. Trench sequences rely on scale and directional audio that collapse at streaming bitrates.

high.title: Oppenheimer
high.rationale: Hoyte van Hoytema 65mm IMAX (colour + b&w), Ludwig Göransson score with aggressive sub-bass. Trinity sequence is a sound-design case study. UHD disc preserves 1.43:1 IMAX framing; streaming doesn't.

high.title: No Country for Old Men
high.rationale: Deakins DP, near-scoreless sound design (the silence IS the tension). Extreme discipline in every craft dimension. The absence of music forces every footstep and breath to carry weight — needs a clean mix to land.

high.title: There Will Be Blood
high.rationale: Robert Elswit photography (Oscar-winning), Jonny Greenwood score with extreme dynamic range, Anderson's long sustained takes. The opening 15 dialogue-free minutes define the register; needs fidelity to read.

high.title: Top Gun: Maverick
high.rationale: Practical aerial photography with actual F-18 cockpit footage, Claudio Miranda DP, strong Atmos mix with engine directionality. Cruise's insistence on practical stunts gives the upgrade something real to reveal.

low.title: Fast Five
low.rationale: User-flagged canonical reference for 'don't bother'. Serviceable image, competent CGI, loud-is-good sound mix. Fun to watch but no fidelity upgrade will make this Scorsese — nothing of craft to reveal beyond what 1080p streaming already delivers.

low.title: The Gray Man
low.rationale: User-flagged canonical reference. Streaming-first action aesthetic — shaky-cam, hyper-cut, colour-graded for phone legibility. Direction by committee, DP assignment was anonymous, no memorable sound moments. Exactly the class of film the upgrade filter should de-prioritise.

low.title: Red Notice
low.rationale: Netflix-originated action-comedy. Flat lighting, CGI-heavy without the craft to back it up, dialogue-over-everything sound mix. Watchable once; no fidelity investment would change the viewing experience.

low.title: Bright
low.rationale: Dim, muddy digital photography (by design; still unhelpful for fidelity). Streaming-native pacing. Nothing in the frame or mix that rewards examination.

low.title: Jurassic World Dominion
low.rationale: Large budget, limited craft. CGI-saturated, score-by-the-numbers, directed to service setpieces rather than cumulative effect. Fidelity will reveal the CGI seams, not hidden depth.

low.title: Transformers: Rise of the Beasts
low.rationale: Loud, busy, indistinguishable from its predecessors at any resolution. The sound mix is compressed for maximum loudness; dynamic range is wasted on the viewer. Upgrade is entirely cosmetic.

low.title: Suicide Squad
low.rationale: Over-graded, chaotic editing, jukebox needle-drop score. Colour and contrast have been ground down by grading passes; no 4K master can recover what was graded out. A film the upgrade filter should explicitly skip.

low.title: Day Shift
low.rationale: Netflix vampire-action. Serviceable-to-poor direction, no DP signature, compressed sound mix. Typical example of streaming-native content with zero fidelity reward.

low.title: The Kissing Booth
low.rationale: Streaming teen-romance. Phone-first framing, flat lighting, dialogue-only mix. No technical dimension rewards investment.

low.title: Murder Mystery 2
low.rationale: Netflix star-vehicle comedy. Flat coverage, no visual identity, sitcom-grade sound mix. The film is indifferent to how you watch it.

low.title: The Mother
low.rationale: Streaming action thriller. Workmanlike direction and DP, no memorable craft moments. Identical experience at 1080p and 4K.

low.title: Rampage
low.rationale: CGI monster movie with competent but indistinguishable craft. Sound is crowd-loudness optimised. Nothing a better encode would recover.

low.title: 6 Underground
low.rationale: Bay at his most maximalist for Netflix. Hyper-cut, over-graded, every frame fighting for attention — nothing to linger on. Sound mix is peak-limited for streaming earbuds. Fidelity reveals seams and colour grading excess, not hidden craft.

low.title: The Adam Project
low.rationale: Netflix star-vehicle with competent but anonymous direction. CGI is fine-for-streaming, DP is uncredited in any meaningful sense. Nothing the 4K encode exposes would change the viewing experience.

low.title: Thunder Force
low.rationale: Netflix superhero comedy. Flat TV lighting, no cinematographer-of-record contribution, compressed sound. A representative example of the streaming-originals class that fidelity investment cannot rescue.

low.title: Army of the Dead
low.rationale: Snyder self-DP; his digital photography is aggressive-but-muddy by design. Sound is dense but flat. Some craft visible (composition) but overall the grading and compression choices work against the upgrade.

low.title: Fast X
low.rationale: Further along the same diminishing-returns curve as Fast 5. Budget is larger, craft thinner. CGI-heavy with visible seams at 4K. Loud mix without layered design.
